concepts.title: Firewalls
concepts.description: Firewalls are network security systems that control incoming and outgoing network traffic based on predetermined security rules. They can be hardware or software-based and are used to prevent unauthorized access to a network or system.

concepts.title: Types of Firewalls
concepts.description: There are two main types of firewalls: Network Firewalls and Host-Based Firewalls. Network Firewalls are hardware or software-based firewalls that protect an entire network, while Host-Based Firewalls are software-based firewalls that protect individual hosts.

concepts.title: Encryption
concepts.description: Encryption is the process of converting plaintext data into unreadable ciphertext to protect it from unauthorized access. It uses a secret key or password to scramble the data, making it unreadable to anyone who doesn't have the key.

concepts.title: Cryptography
concepts.description: Cryptography is the practice of secure communication in the presence of third-party adversaries. It involves using algorithms and protocols to protect data from unauthorized access, interception, or modification.

concepts.title: Risk Management
concepts.description: Students often confuse Risk Management with Threat Management. Risk Management involves identifying, assessing, and mitigating risks to an organization's assets, while Threat Management involves identifying and mitigating specific threats to an organization's assets.

concepts.title: Types of Network Threats
concepts.description: There are several types of network threats, including Malware, Viruses, Trojan Horses, Spyware, Adware, and Ransomware. Each type of threat has different characteristics and requires different mitigation strategies.

concepts.title: Security Protocols
concepts.description: Some important security protocols include SSL/TLS, SSH, and VPN. These protocols provide secure communication over the internet and protect data from interception or eavesdropping.

concepts.title: Access Control
concepts.description: Access Control involves controlling who has access to a network, system, or resource. It involves identifying users, authenticating users, and authorizing users to access specific resources.
